food_subgroup: Sweetened tapioca pearls / bubble tea components

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tapioca pearl entry (FOODON_03311737); no specific black-sugar-coated tapioca pearl entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tapioca/cassava starch products. No specific black-sugar bubble pearl code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for black sugar tapioca pearls as a distinct food item.

serving.description: 1 serving of black sugar bubble pearls (as a dessert or topping)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of black sugar bubble pearls is approximately 80 g, equivalent to a standard portion served as a dessert or added as a topping to bubble tea or tang shui.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca pearls (cooked, sweetened) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with brown sugar composition data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for black-sugar-coated tapioca pearls exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked tapioca pearl data (FDC ID 169717, tapioca, pearl, dry — adjusted for cooked state) combined with brown sugar syrup contribution. Energy and carbohydrate values reflect the high sugar load from black sugar simmering. Protein and fat are negligible as tapioca starch and brown sugar contain essentially no protein or fat. Sodium is low as no salt is added in traditional preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for tapioca/cassava-based starch products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black-sugar-coated tapioca pearls. GI estimated at 70 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tapioca pudding (GI ~70, glucose reference) and cooked tapioca starch products. Black sugar (brown sugar) has a GI of approximately 65–70. The combined product is estimated at GI 70 (high). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (70 × 43.0 × 0.80) / 100 = 24.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Black sugar bubble pearls are very low in sodium and do not contribute meaningfully to hypertensive risk; however, portion control is still advisable given the high sugar content, which may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) and high carbohydrate load from tapioca starch and black sugar syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid black sugar bubble pearls due to their high GI (~70) and high glycemic load (~24 per serving), which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (~22 g per 100 g) contributing to excess caloric intake and potential dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high free sugar content of black sugar bubble pearls may contribute to elevated triglycerides and caloric surplus when consumed regularly, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Sugar Bubble Pearls (黑糖珍珠, Hēi táng zhēnzhū) are chewy, caramelised tapioca pearls that have been slowly simmered in traditional black sugar (a form of unrefined brown sugar with a deep molasses character) until each pearl is fully infused with a rich, dark sweetness. The pearls are made from tapioca starch derived from cassava, shaped into small spheres, boiled until soft and translucent, then cooked further in black sugar syrup through a multi-step reduction process that coats each pearl in a glossy, amber-coloured caramel layer. The texture is characteristically soft, chewy, and slightly sticky, with a flavour profile that is sweet, mildly caramel-like, and earthy from the unrefined sugar. In Singapore, black sugar bubble pearls are commonly served as a dessert component in tang shui (Chinese sweet soup) establishments, as a topping for shaved ice, or as the signature pearl in black sugar bubble tea drinks. Nutritionally, the pearls are almost entirely carbohydrate-based, with negligible protein and fat. The high tapioca starch and added sugar content results in a high glycemic index (estimated ~70) and a high glycemic load per serving (~24), making them a high-glycaemic food. They are naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and vegan. Clinically, they are of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome due to their rapid glucose-raising potential. Sodium content is very low, posing minimal hypertensive risk. Regular or large-portion consumption may contribute to excess caloric intake and elevated triglycerides, relevant to cardiovascular health.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for black-sugar-coated tapioca pearls was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA cooked tapioca pearl data with brown sugar contribution estimated from USDA brown sugar composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database tapioca pudding data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-understood and the adaptation is scientifically defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central cooked tapioca pearl data (FDC ID 169717) combined with brown sugar syrup contribution; no exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for tapioca-based products. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 5. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 6. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 7. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported; tapioca and brown sugar contain no B12 by nature (naturally zero by food type, but reported as -1 as the source does not explicitly confirm). 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported; naturally zero for plant-based starch products but source does not explicitly confirm. 12. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 13. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 14. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 15. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 16. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 17. GI is an analogous estimate at 70 (high) based on University of Sydney GI Database tapioca pudding entry; no direct measurement for black sugar pearls exists. 18. This food is classified under Desserts rather than Mixed to better reflect its primary use as a sweet dessert component. 19. The food is served by RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 at Funan Mall, Singapore, per field survey source data.
